comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. If that doesn't help, consider remedial reading classes; yes, it really is as simple and straightforward as the FAQ says. 

The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineages in photos. Juliet Ashley, whose photo featured in the Magic Monday three weeks ago, was a student of at least four significant occult teachers, and this is the third of them, the grand old man of 20th century American occultism, Manly P. Hall. Born in Canada in 1901, Hall emigrated to the US with his grandmother in 1904, took to occultism in his teens, and moved to Los Angeles, where he spent most of the rest of his life. Juliet Ashley was a student and supporter of Hall's, and helped fund the gorgeous first edition of The Secret Teachings of All Ages, the book that made his reputation. If you know Hall's teachings, you'll find them all through the material I received from John Gilbert. 


[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Magical Name
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

In Applied Magic Dion Fortune says that one's magical name can be given one by one's teacher or can be discovered by oneself. Some websites say that "Dion Fortune" was her magical name. Yet she herself describes the magical name as being composed of "barbarous syllables". So it doesn’t seem that "Dion Fortune" was her magical name. She says about the magical name: "The psychology of the uses of the magical name needs more study than I can give it at the moment without digression. It must suffice to say that its uses are traditional and I have proved its efficacy in practice."

Is there a little more that you can say about the magical name and its uses, and if you yourself use one?

Thanks, Vermilion Addlepated Banshee

replies.title: Re: Magical Name
replies.content: Her magical motto -- that is, the name she went by in Golden Dawn circles -- was Deo Non Fortuna, "God, not luck" in Latin; like many Golden Dawnies in the class-conscious Britain of her time, she used the motto on her family coat of arms. She mentions in another essay that she used her magical name to formulate her astral body for astral projection, and that's doubtless one of the things she was talking about.

comments.title: astrological talismans (Saturn, and a practical question); reincarnation
comments.content:
Good morning JMG,

And thank you very much for another Magic Monday, and for the insight into spiritual traditions. The guys (and gals) on these photos all have a very nice smile - that‘s probably the best advertisement you could make for following an occult path… :-)

1. About astrological talismans, especially in relation to Saturn:

Would there be any problem if I created astrological Saturn talismans for somebody else, even though my own Saturn is negatively afflicted? Or anything to look out for when I do?

And yes, I will do divination before starting with this project ;-), and also invoke him in an appropriate way on a few Saturdays to establish a relationship - I just wanted to get a general idea about potential pitfalls and things to watch out for especially in relation to Saturn.


2. To you and everybody else, a practical question about talismans in general:

I want to use appropriate stones as the basis for future talismans, e.g. maybe obsidian for Saturn. But I don‘t have the skills or equipment to engrave them with the relevant drawings or text. So my current working plan is to draw/write on the stones with a suitable pen, but I assume that this might wear off over time, and will also probably look like a child‘s work, especially given my level of practical artistical skills.

Does anybody have any experience with getting designs on stones/polished minerals? Any other ideas or suggestions what I could do, or what techniques to use?

Or anybody who has done stone engravings before and can tell me how steep the learning curve is before I invest in any equipment?

Any ideas or suggestions appreciated.


3. And, if I may, one last and unrelated question:

You‘ve mentioned before that Mozart is a prime example for reincarnation, in how quickly he picked up on musical skills learned in a previous life. Yet quite a few of us will have learned something like reading or writing in a previous life, but most of us don‘t pick up a book or a pen at age two and just hit the road running. ;-)

So what does it take to remember skills or knowledge from a previous life?

Or rather, more interestingly, is there a way to deliberately remember (or speed up remembering) of knowledge and skills from a previous life, if one suspected having learned something earlier on?

Thanks, as always, and may you and your wife have a blessed week!

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: astrological talismans (Saturn, and a practical question); reincarnation
replies.content:
2. I recently undertook a project to construct my Druid stones and received some hard won wisdom while doing so. Somehow there is no manual on making stone talismans and our esteemed host basically told me to „get a rock“. :-) I also had it in mind, that I would make stone talismans. I would break the process down to 3 stages:
a. (optional) if you are not satisfied with the raw stone shape, you can mild them, well grind them down as the case may be. I cut raw milky quartz with an angle grinder and a marble grinding steel file for it. One does have to get some skill with it, but it is possible.
b. Polishing; (optional) I wanted a nice smooth finish, so I used sand paper to grind the sides down. Start with 60 or 80 grain. You do not need water grinding for the first stage even if doing it with a machine. But further grains you should grind down with a wett tool. I used 60 grain, then 240, 600 and finally 1200 grain. One can go to a mirror polish up to 10.000 grain, but for that I would need specialised tools. And the aqua-sandpaper up to 1200 grain I could get at the local hardware shop.
c. Writing. A normal pencil leaves temporary marks, but I wanted something permanent. So I bought a dremel. Look online, mine costed about 60$ + some diamond tipped bits for stone. I drew a symbol with a pencil and then drew along with the tip of the dremel a 1mm grove. It is much simpler then it sounds.
d. Colouring. I did not want to just leave it like that, so I painted the insides of the groves. You will want a none aqua acrylic paint. Go to a local arts and crafts shop, or a local school supplies shop. The paints were landed for metal, stone or ceramic. They should have what you need. I think I got about 12 paints, including metal paints and appropriate fine tipped brushes for about 12$.
e. Enchanting….

Hope this helps. I can elaborate if you need. Best regards, V

P.S. JMG. If per chance the question arises; Is it unwise to post a picture of one’s enchanted Druid stones online, if one is currently actively using them?

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Banishing has been a concern for me since I began systematic occult work and often, during the imaginative component of banishing each gate in the SoP, there are wispy traces, flecks, etc. that seem to dig in their heels the harder I try to send them off after the bulk of the crud is already gone; they seem to have reflexes or simple minds of their own. At various times I've dealt with this by straining during ritual, resulting in inconvenient 25-minute SoPs. It occurred to me tonight however, that good banishing might be pasteurization rather than sterilization. When you sterilize a medium, say, straw for growing edible mushrooms, the grow will fail unless maintained in sterile surroundings, as certain kinds of contaminants have an advantage when there's no pre-existing microbial community; if one pasteurizes the straw, on the other hand, it's not completely clean, but the mycelium you're introducing has the trophic advantage even if the straw is left in a dirty environment; the remaining microbes have concerns unrelated to yours and outcompete those likely to ruin your project. Whatever irksome astral phenomena I needed to strain to drive off in order to achieve that very clean feeling I sought from ritual--which, come to think of it, might not fall within the natural range of fluctuation for human consciousness (in the same way sterility does not occur in the wild)--are like unhelpful microbes that thrive on sterile straw; but if one banishes merely those manifestations of an element the element in question is capable of resorbing under the influence of an unstrained yet reasonably directed will, you don't feel squeaky clean, but whatever work you intend to follow up the banishing with still has passably neat conditions under which to proceed. The question in this is, is it better to shoot for a more modest change in consciousness with banishing rituals rather than the pure state I was going for? I now wonder whether it may have been excessive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Or, might the wisps be like microbes that are naturally left over after pasteurization, and sending them away might create a fertile environment for imbalances I'm not as aware of?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Aim for a more modest change. It's as though you're doing repeated sterilizations; even if any one sterilization has incomplete effects, the cumulative effect of repeated practice is considerable.

comments.title: Harm of New Age Goddess worship
comments.content:
You are probably aware many women in the west, typically the more educated and well off with feminist views, took to what's known as godess worship. A lot of times they see "the godess" as a symbol and abstraction of feminism, without realizing the spirituality of it all.

I am reminded of your views of how mainstream meditation is practiced, and "what can go wrong". My question is, do you think the same applies here? Do you think there are potentially harmful consequences to bringing back the worship of long forgotten godesses, some of which, judging by their, mythology are not always nice and sometimes do things not suited to modern feminist sensibilities, or is this just a harmless religion with retreats for those with too much money to spend?

replies.title: Re: Harm of New Age Goddess worship
replies.content:
It's a very mixed bag. On the upside, there are plenty of people who've come to think, as some Pagans like to say, that a religion without goddesses is halfway to atheism, and find that approaching the Divine through feminine symbols works better in their spiritual lives. That makes perfect sense to me.

On the other hand, a lot of the people who are engaged in feminist goddess worship are simply worshipping themselves -- have you see the bumper stickers that say, "Back Off, I'm A Goddess"? Anthropolatry, the worship of human beings by themselves, has very serious downsides; in particular, it leads to the kind of ego inflation that makes people incapable of learning from their mistakes or paying attention when the universe tells them that they're wrong. Blind rage in response to anything that doesn't go their way is a typical reaction of people caught up in this sort of toxic thinking.

As for ancient goddesses, most goddesses -- like most gods -- can be invoked without undue danger, but there are exceptions. There are exceptions, however, and some goddesses are definitely included. Those feminist goddess worshippers who assume that any deity who is notionally female is by definition safe, nurturing, and harmless may be in for an unwelcome surprise or two...

replies.title: Re: Harm of New Age Goddess worship
replies.content:
Not surprisingly for an occult topic, there have been reams of nonsense written about goddesses and goddess worship. A number of books tend to run deities together, even across miles of geography. So much was written along the lines of "The people of this south-Asian island have a fish goddess named Gleb; the people of this Inuit village worship a walrus goddess named Glabah; they are obviously the same great guardian of the waters." Also the assumption that all goddesses are some form of great mother---Uh, what part of Artemis and Athena were not mothers did you not notice? A number of people have also picked up the rather personal mythology of the poet Robert Graves as displayed in _The White Goddess_. If you also read his essays, poetry and biography you will realized that the book is heavily influenced by the author's own psychosexual hang-ups. Also by the fact that he was analyzing a Welsh poem without reading Welsh.

But deciding that a deity will be who you want them to be is not new. I recently read _Inanna: Lady of Largest Heart_ by Betty De Shong Meador, which gives translations of hymns and poems to Inanna, the Sumerian goddess. Meador presents evidence that the author, Enheduanna, who was a priestess of the moon god, wrote these poems out of her personal experience of the goddess and with an intent of magnifying the power and role of the goddess in her society. She seems to have been successful as her hymns were set practice pieces for students learning to write, accounting for the number of copies that survived. So, someone over 6000 years ago was capable of deciding that her favorite deity was more important than others believed and working to spread the idea. Being a Sargon's daughter probably helped

Lotte Motz' _Faces of the Goddess_ argues that the idea of great mother goddess is a modern concept. She claims that the great anxieties of early humans were food and protection from danger, not human fertility. She examines a spectrum of cultures that have goddesses but claims not to have found any that fulfil the roles assigned by those who assumed an early matriarchy and worship directed to a goddess seen as the mother or source of all living things. Worth reading.

On the subject of goddess invocation in modern times there is an amusing story of a coven of witches worshipping in the nude, as several traditions do, who decided to specifically invoke Athena into their priestess. She arrived, angrily asked, "Why am I NAKED?" and departed. So, take the trouble to learn before you invoke.

Arguments over the roles and powers of different deities are not limited to goddesses of course. Witness the ongoing discussions among modern Heathens over the role of Loki.

I think that feminist spirituality, including the rediscovery of goddess worship, was an important step for many women in the 70s. In addition there were and are many women who worked to change the religions of their upbringing as in opening Christian ministries to women, Jewish women becoming rabbis, and so forth. Gus di Zerega examines this reopening of Western culture to the influence of the female in _Fault Lines: the Sixties, the Culture War and the Return of the Divine Feminine_"

Rita

replies.content:

comments.title: Mentalism
comments.content:
Dear JMG

I think this qualifies as a magic related question so putting it here.

Do you know of any good resources to learn the skills that old school mentalists/mind readers had? I'm thinking of stage magicians, not ritual magic.

I'm a fan of the (present day) British stage magician Derren Brown who has done a number of very high profile TV specials (he plays real Russian Roulette with a gun in one, convinces someone to push someone off a roof in another, convinces someone else to save an aeroplane, guesses lottery numbers on live TV and so on).

The skills appear to be a mix of incredible memory, hypnosis, suggestion, and absolutely preternatural skill at reading facial expressions and body language and so on - all developed to a very high degree with a strong dose of showmanship added in.

He always emphasises there is nothing supernatural about what he does and he's a committed rationalist (debunks fake psychics on tv etc) but what he does is astonishing.

He's written his own book called Tricks of the Mind:

https://www.amazon.co.uk/Tricks-Mind-Derren-Brown/dp/1905026358/ref=sr_1_1?crid=8WPSZDN21G6

It's been a few years since I read it and it has some interesting stuff but nothing about what the most interesting aspect of his skills (to me) - the way he can read people like a book. One of his standard tricks is to guess what people are thinking by simply standing in front of them and observing them and their body language when they replay the memory in their mind) - in one example I saw, he accurately guessed that the man was remembering a childhood memory where he had run into a stand of brambles and thorns in a field and hurt himself.

I know there's plenty of stuff on memory skills that you've written about (and so has he in his book), and there's stuff on hypnosis etc but do you know of anything written to build all the skills of the old school stage mentalists of the 1920s/30s and earlier which he's replicating now? Kind of similar to how you can find books by Max Alding about the old school strongmen?

And specifically about the astonishing body language/"mind reading" skills he has - I've not seen any resources on learning to read people that well?

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content:
Take a look at:

https://www.deceptionary.com/aboutreading.html

Of the many books mentioned there, I would recommend those by Ray Hyman, Robert Alan Nelson, Myriam Ruthchild and Richard Webster. Herb Dewey and Bascom Jones are highly esteemed in general, but I haven't read their works myself.

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content: Thank you Robert and everyone else!

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content: "The Law of Suggestion" by Santanelli is a good one for old school hypnosis...

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content:
A useful resource for learning about body language, though it's not directly focused on applying the knowledge, is the Online Nonverbal Language Dictionary: https://center-for-nonverbal-studies.org/

Also collected into a book published a few years back: https://www.alibris.com/The-Routledge-Dictionary-of-Nonverbal-Communication-David-B-Givens/book/51390057?matches=15

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content: I'm glad some of the commentariat answered this because it's a field about which I know precisely nothing!

replies.title: Re: Mentalism
replies.content:
One thing to keep in mind is that just because a stage magic performer chooses not to claim supernatural powers, it doesn't mean they're being honest about how they actually do perform their tricks. Pretending that the method they're using is something much more impressive than the actual method is quite common. For instance, if a card magician, either during a performance or in an interview, claims that some trick requires them to quickly memorize the order of all the cards in a randomly shuffled deck, they might not be lying (it's just within the realm of possibility) but they probably are. That's part of the showmanship.

Stage mentalists often claim they're reading precise details from body language alone, using subtle yet perfectly effective suggestion cues to control people, and other exotic but unlikely methods. It ain't necessarily so. Their performances require great skill, but as with all stage magic one of the main skills is deception.

Good AFternoon!

To our American friends, a very happy Labor Day! Thanks as always to our host for this wonderful resource, and to the commentariat for sharing their knowledge.

To Share: For anyone interested in Germanic mythology and/or poetry, I was introduced this past week to "Hrafnagaldur Odins", which means something like "Raven Magic Song of Odin". It's a poem that was long included in the Poetic Edda, but more recently, it's dating has been controversial, and it seems that most scholars conclude from some internal clues that it was more likely a consciously antiquarian composition from the 16th or 17th century. Whatever its date, it has some interesting takes on several Gods and Goddesses - what I have so far found most interesting is an implied link between Idun and the world-tree Yggdrasil and possibly also with the Norns. I expect it to provide many fine themes for meditation down the road. You can find the complete text in Old Norse with a scholarly translation, along with pretty extensive commentary in this PDF. ;

To Ask: Not exactly esoteric, but do you have any opinion on Graham Hancock's works? I read Fingerprints of the Gods years back and at the time found the arguments pretty convincing. Afterwards, I read some skeptical materialist takedowns of his work that I found convincing then, and now that I'm re-evaluating many such things, I'm wondering if it'll be worthwhile to re-visit his stuff.

Thanks very much,
Jeff

I also think sometimes that I'm dealing with trauma from more than one life, like maybe I could've weathered the trauma of this life better if I wasn't already weakened from something before. I don't know for certain, I have no memories (and don't want them at this point), but it feels right. A lifetime focused on healing might be what I need. If so, I haven't done a great job of it so far (getting tangled in NeoPaganism before finding my way here certainly didn't help).

Do you have any additional advice on finding gods to invoke? I've done the route of reading mythology, but I don't find myself drawn to anything in particular, nothing jumps out at me. I know one of the lasting legacies of my bad childhood is a strong sense of what I don't want and a very weak sense of what I do want, and I must be able to reasonably think I can have the thing before I can let myself want it (the only reason I got asked what I wanted was so other people knew what to threaten or take away, in turn I tried to keep that category empty). I don't really want to give up on it altogether, but I'm not sure what else I can do.

Solving Psychic Problems: Several classes and types of human beings are drawn to religious study and membership in religious organizations. Generally speaking, these potential members or followers come from more introverted levels of mental and emotional activity. This is not intended to imply any defect of mind, as most of world's original and creative thinkers have been more or less introverted. It does, however, imply a personality pattern which under certain stress or strain is subject to a remarkable intensification of the imaginative faculty and a proclivity to daydreaming. An intense internal life and a limitation of external interests distinguish those most likely to be interested in abstract religious or mystical research and speculation.

Humans are also divided into defensives and escapists. The defense mechanism is a subconscious instinct to protect the ego from what the poet calls the arrows of outrageous fortune. The individual builds attitudes and convictions as buffers against realities. The tendency may be so subtle that only a very honest and thoughtful survey of characteristics and reactions can reveal the mechanism. The escapist lives in a world of substitutions. He constantly pacifies his dissatisfactions by imposing upon them a sequence of secret hopes, dreams and visions of personality -and appetite- fulfillment in some remote and more congenial time or place. Here, again, the thoughtful individual must examine himself to determine the extent and power of these equations in his own nature. We are most likely to be deceived by conditions around us if first we have in some way deceived ourselves.

Psychological phenomena are almost always associated with long-established personality pressures. To the degree that we lose the ability to relax, we open ourselves to the conflicts which tension invariably causes. There is a delicate consideration involved in all processes of self-analysis. The human being, especially one under pressure, is apt to be an extremist, even when attempting to analyze his own characteristics. The flood of literature dealing with popular psychology and psychiatry for the layman has contributed to the very dilemma which it was intended to relieve. Critical self-analysis, in terms of an array of frightening descriptions, warnings, symptoms, etc., may cause new pressures, fears, worries, and doubts. Even the professional psychologist is not always comforting, and unless he is better than the average he can confound the existing confusion. But even so, a simple and direct survey of our own policies can be both helpful and revealing.

comments.title: Magical Properties of Hickory
comments.content:
I had an unusual occurrence a couple of weeks back. At my place of work, which right now is a tire and auto repair place (I'm part-timing it for some extra cash) a staff-sized piece of wood showed up. It was left leaning against the window next to the door. This was about mid-day. It stood there for about an hour, as I walked past it several times. Eventually, seeing that no one else was claiming it, or even acting as if they noticed it, I took it.

Here's a picture of it:
[IMAGE:0]

It's about 65" (1.6 meters) tall and fairly straight. That's a meter stick next to it in one picture, and close-ups of the wood. It shows signs of being cut and it's dried out like it's not fresh but has been somewhere out of the weather. It is a very dense wood. Questions to people in one of my occult FB groups think it is hickory.

That's not a wood that shows up in a casual search for its magical properties or associations. Native American mythology sheds some substantial light on the magic of the hickory. The Seneca discusses hickory trees at length in their mythology (as described in Seneca Indian Myths, collected by Jeremiah Curtin). The hickory is featured prominently in these myths and is connected with the dead and bringing the dead to life.

“Hodadeio and His Sister” the bones of the dead are placed before a great hickory tree and, typically, the living person who placed the bones pushes against the hickory and shouts “Rise up or the tree will fall on you” the dead rise up and become living again."

I'm going to work it into a staff eventually, once I get more info and do more research.

I was wondering what anyone knows, or their opinions. Thoughts on the wood too.

comments.title: Care with Mesoamerican deities
comments.content:
I've seen some interest rising for the past decade in the traditions of the mesoamerican cultures, which makes me happy, naturally, but I'm getting a little worried about their misuse because of improper information that started since the Conquest until this day. I will be writing a post about this, as part of a series of mexican shamanism that I am writing once a month about. It's a decently sized comment so I left it for the end but if you want me to repost for visibility, as there was some justified concern a while back I can do that.

Sorry if this is a little bit alarming, but I wanted to share here one way to look at it. The Aztec pantheon, which derives from the Toltecs and before through the Mayas assigns deity to almost everything, it is generous in that sense, but that also means they work and blend together. That also means that gods like Tezcatlipoca a trickster god, are assigned a very powerful force of nature corresponding to planes that seem to be the same as elementals and Elemental Kings. Which require a lot of care. You don't want to work with the god that fooled Quetzalcoatl; that is called The Smoking Mirror; and which's cult was one of the greatest promotors of human sacrifice and black magic (not all cults were) in mesoamerican societies without very strict guidance. Only the ones that cannot be fooled by themselves and by others should even attempt it, I do not know any living practitioner (though there might be) in Mexico that does it successfully and these are advanced shamans. For example, one way you are chosen as "hailer" --a shaman that controls the weather from my native state-- is for a lightning bolt to fall upon you, and yes, many have died.

One of your readers mentioned a vision of Tonatiuh (possibly) in a dream, the god of Fifth Sun, a few MM's back and he mentioned, to my relief, that it was very powerful indeed and easy to loose control. I can see how that can go wrong without the proper preparation and the balancing of an Earth goddess like Cihuacóatl(the goddess of the material body, reincarnation and carnal love) or Centéotl (the goddess of maize, of which humans are made off according to mesoamerican mythology) which the Aztecs were experts at, at some point.

Above all do not mix both. Messing badly enough with the god of the Fifth Sun mixed with Tezcatlipoca might turn you into an egotistical, impossible, self-delusional, sacrificial murderer. Someone commented on my blog that a friend of his was getting similar ideas, and thought I would mention it.

With that said, if someone is interested in the mesoamerican pantheons, philosophy and magic more than suggest a particular deity or path, I suggest to establish a good and frequent relationship with a known deity from the Christian, Greek, Norse or Druid Revival pantheons and ask for help and guidance while you read literature about mesoamerican theology and culture. That's what I did and it worked.

Being a Mexican and a recent member of one the current traditions I also want to share a less extremist vision of the same spirit but manifested for our age, with a song from a Mexican band named High Magic translated below. It shows a different way to think about sacrifice and true love the heart. It's great to pray over to establish a connection. You can almost tell why Christianity was well adopted, and later syncretized.

Great Spirit

Great Spirit, Great Meemaw, Great Papaw
I present as myself before you
As I am, I ask you for thy blessing
As I’m grateful for the heart you’ve placed in me

Great Spirit, Great Opa, Great Oma
I present as myself before you
As I am, I ask you for thy blessing
I am grateful for the heart you’ve place in me

When I come I just come. I just come
Great Spirit its before thee my coming
To surrender my heart before you
Heart as the only thing I own

jura yandaje’ yandacóa!
cure and heal…
cure and heal this blessing you’ve given me!
cure and heal…
restore and care for the love you’ve placed in me!
cura yandaje’ uyawanka tanka!

Great Spirit. Great grandfather, Great grandmother…
I present myself before you
As I am, I ask you for thy blessing
As I’m grateful for the heart you’ve placed in me

And this is some music inspired on temazcales, they chant similar songs during temazcales --mesoamerican ritual steam baths so to speak, on a clay "womb". https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_r7pwQ4N0lg

comments.content:
You mentioned before that if your life has a predetermined purpose, you'll know it very early in life. This raises a lot of very strange memories and ideas from childhood, which, when paired with the current global mess, seems quite ominous. Simply put, I've always had a sense, for as long as I could remember, that my life would be defined by a quest to try to rebuild the world I grew up in (I was born in 1995, so let's call it the early 21st century). I've always known, somehow, that the world I live in will not last, it will soon end and be quite decisively replaced by something else, and that I am going to need to do everything I can to try to rebuild the positive elements of the lost world.

If this is the case, then it follows that the other things I've known to be true will also hold: and one of them in particular has always deeply troubled me: I've known my entire life that everyone I know will be dead and unable to help. My question is simple: if my sense of life's purpose is accurate, does this sort of knowledge also come through?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It really varies from case to case. Sometimes the knowledge that comes through is symbolic, not literal -- it may simply be that certain people who might otherwise have helped you may be dead by then.
